foodServiceApplicability: Miami food preparation establishments and other non-residential facilities that handle, prepare, or process food and can discharge fats, oils, or grease to the sanitary sewer must obtain and maintain a Miami-Dade FOG Discharge Control operating permit.
interceptorType: Miami-Dade regulates grease traps and grease interceptors through permit conditions, installation and maintenance guidance, and DERM-permitted facility recordkeeping for FOG generators.
pumpOutFrequency: Miami-Dade's public materials say operators must follow the cleaning and pump-out frequency written into the GDO permit and maintenance guidance, keep the log current for every service event, and react when overflow or near-solid grease conditions show the device is no longer working properly.
manifestRequirement: Keep the grease trap or interceptor maintenance log near the device, record every FOG removal or maintenance action, and keep all pump-out and maintenance receipts available at the facility for a minimum of three years.
submissionMethod: Start from Miami-Dade's DERM-permitted liquid waste transporter workflow, use the current transporter list or request the current grease-trap hauler list from DERM, and retain the hauler receipts with the on-site maintenance log.
enforcementNote: Miami-Dade ties FOG compliance to an annual operating permit, warns that overflows can trigger fines and health risks, and checks the maintenance log and receipts during DERM inspections.
